questions.description: Answer (a) and (b) (a) Máire is a slightly overweight woman who sees the… Company.
questions.text: Answer (a) and (b) (a) Máire is a slightly overweight woman who sees the following advertisement in the newspaper: "The Shrink Delight Diet - Weight Loss While You Wait! Drink a Shrink Delight Milkshake for Breakfast, a Shrink Delight Soup for Lunch and have a sensible dinner and watch your weight drop! Shrink Delight Ltd is so confident in this product that if you follow the Shrink Delight Diet and don't lose 10 kilograms within 6 weeks, we will give you €2,000!" Máire buys a 6 week supply of Shrink Delight Milkshakes and Shrink Delight Soups. She drinks the milkshake for breakfast and the soup for lunch every day, and has what she regards as a sensible dinner every evening. However, after 6 weeks she has not lost any weight. When she contacts Shrink Delight Ltd, they refuse to give her the €2,000, saying that Máire should not have taken the advertisement seriously. Advise Máire on whether she has a claim in contract against Shrink Delight Ltd for the €2,000. [AND]{.underline} (b) James is the manager of Machine Ltd, which is involved in the business of buying and selling large machinery for use in the construction industry. On January 15th, James received a phone call from Todd, the manager of a large construction company, Construction Ltd. Todd asked James to post him a quotation for a XL Super Crane. James posted a letter to Todd, offering to sell the crane for €50,000. The letter stated that the offer was subject to Machine Ltd's terms and conditions, which were included with the letter. Term 6 was a price variation clause which provided that the cost of the machine could be increased by up to 10% in the event of changes in market conditions. Todd replied to this offer by sending James an order form by post, using Construction Ltd's own standard order form. The price of the crane was stated to be €50,000, but the order form contained its own set of terms and conditions, which did not contain a price variation clause. James signed and returned by post a portion of Construction Ltd's order form which acknowledged that the contract took place on the purchaser's terms. However, he included a covering letter which stated that the sale was to take place on Machine Ltd's terms and conditions. The XL Crane was delivered on March 15th and James sent an invoice for €55,000, referring to a change in market conditions and Term 6 of the contract as an explanation for the increase in price. Construction Ltd is refusing to pay the extra €5,000. Advise James.

questions.description: John goes shopping in Dublin city centre every Saturday and regularly… Negligenc.
questions.text: John goes shopping in Dublin city centre every Saturday and regularly parks his car in the O'Connell Street car park in the centre of Dublin. This is a "Pay and Display'' car park, meaning that each week John first parks his car, then pays for his parking at a machine and receives a parking ticket, which he leaves visible in the window of his car before going shopping. There is a sign above the pay machine headed "Terms and Conditions of Parking". The sign contains 20 clauses in relatively small print. The 12th term excludes liability "in contract or tort or any other action" for "any damage to property or persons howsoever caused" while cars are left in the car park. One Saturday John parks his car in the O'Connell Street car park and pays for his ticket as normal, but when he returns to his car a large light falls from the ceiling of the car park onto his car. John sustains cuts and bruises, and the windscreen of his car is smashed. It transpires that the light fell as a result of the negligence of the car park staff. However, Carla, the Manager of the car park, claims that John cannot bring an action against the owners of the car park in either contract or tort for his injuries or the damage to his car because of clause 12 of the "Terms and Conditions of Parking". Advise John on whether clause 12 has been incorporated into his contract, and, assuming it is incorporated, whether it can prevent him from bringing an action against the owners of the car park.

questions.description: Jane runs a catering business from her home, and Christmas is one of her… Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Jane runs a catering business from her home, and Christmas is one of her busiest times of year as she caters for a lot of office Christmas parties. In early October she orders 50 turkeys from Pat, a local turkey farmer. The turkeys are to be delivered on 1st December. Jane explains to Pat that she always serves turkey as the main course at Christmas parties. However, on 1st December Pat phones to say that he won't be able to deliver the turkeys. Jane is distraught. She cannot get turkeys from any other source, because it is too late in the season. She has to cancel 10 parties. She would have made a final profit of €3,000 had she not had to cancel these parties. As a result of cancelling these parties she also cannot make use of other ingredients she has purchased and she has wasted advertising costs. In total she has a wasted expenditure of €2,000. The whole incident is causing her a lot of stress and inconvenience, and early in the New Year her doctor prescribes her tranquilisers to help her cope. Jane wants damages to cover her lost profits and her wasted expenditure, as well as damages to cover the stress and inconvenience. Pat admits that he is in breach of contract, but refuses to pay damages to Jane. To help her relax after this ordeal, Jane decides to have a Jacuzzi installed in her home. She enters into a contract with Tony, whereby Tony agrees to supply and install a Jacuzzi three metres long and one metre deep in Jane's bathroom, at a cost of €1,000. However, when the Jacuzzi is installed, Jane is annoyed because it is only two and a half metres long and ninety centimetres deep. Other than the difference in size, the Jacuzzi is perfectly useable and has all of the features Jane requires. Jane wants damages of €3,000 to cover the cost of tearing out the Jacuzzi that has been installed and replacing it with a properly installed, bigger Jacuzzi. She wants further damages for the stress and inconvenience of getting the Jacuzzi replaced. Tony admits that he is in breach of contract, but he is refusing to pay damages. He says that the difference in the size of the Jacuzzi is only minor, and that the Jacuzzi installed is still worth at least €900. He suspects that Jane has no intention of actually replacing the installed Jacuzzi but instead wants to use the money to go on holidays. Advise Jane.

questions.description: In January 2008, Mel rents an office from Well Ltd agreeing to pay an… Company.
questions.text: In January 2008, Mel rents an office from Well Ltd agreeing to pay an annual rent of €55,000 subject to a rent review on 30th December 2013. The rent review clause provides that on that date the rent should reflect the current market rental value as agreed between parties. In December 2013 the market rental value of the office is €80,000. Finn, a senior manager in Well Ltd, writes to Mel suggesting a figure of €52,000 for the new rent. Mel writes back readily agreeing to renew upon the terms proposed. Finn then realises his mistake and writes to Mel explaining that he had intended to propose a rent of €85,000 and asking her to treat his letter as if it had suggested a review at that figure. Mel informs Finn that she will not agree to the correction proposed and that she considers that a binding agreement exists. While accepting that Finn has authority to act on its behalf, Well Ltd argues that Finn's mistake invalidates the agreement. Well Ltd is unhappy with Finn's performance and it decides his annual salary of €50,000 could be put to better use. In January 2013, Finn is dismissed and Well Ltd pays him compensation of €150,000. In February 2013, it is discovered that Finn has engaged in insider dealing in breach of his service contract and in breach of company law. It becomes clear that at the time it terminated his contract, Well Ltd could have dismissed Finn for breach without having to pay compensation. Well Ltd argues that the termination contract is void and the compensation repayable. Advise Well Ltd. Please note that your answer should be limited to the contractual issues which arise in this question.

questions.description: Answer (a) and (b) With reference to relevant authorities, briefly explain the difference between and a term and a…
questions.text: Answer (a) and (b) With reference to relevant authorities, briefly explain the difference between and a term and a representation. [AND]{.underline} With reference to relevant authorities, briefly explain the difference between a warranty, a condition and an innominate (or intermediate) term.

questions.description: Discuss the impact of EU directives and current harmonisation initiatives on Irish contract law.
questions.text: Discuss the impact of EU directives and current harmonisation initiatives on Irish contract law.

questions.description: With reference to relevant authority, discuss the rule in Pinnel's case and discuss whether promissory estoppel can…
questions.text: With reference to relevant authority, discuss the rule in Pinnel's case and discuss whether promissory estoppel can provide a remedy to the party (debtor) who has relied on the promise of the creditor to accept a lesser sum in satisfaction of a larger debt.

questions.description: Answer (a) or (b) (a) Discuss the effect of frustration of contract.
questions.text: Answer (a) or (b) (a) Discuss the effect of frustration of contract. In your answer discuss (i) whether any money paid in advance of the frustrating event must be returned [and]{.underline} (ii) whether any work done before the frustrating event must be paid for. OR (b) Discuss the circumstances in which a contract will be declared invalid on the grounds of economic duress.

questions.description: Aoife is an internationally recognised collector of Waterford crystal. Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Aoife is an internationally recognised collector of Waterford crystal. She reads in the magazine "Crystal" of an auction of "fine crystal" at Kelly's Auctions in Dublin. She attends the viewing the day before the auction. She meets Seán, a recently retired glass-blower in the Waterford crystal factory. Seán explains his background and tells her that in order to bolster his pension he has decided to sell his entire personal collection of glasses in one lot, Lot 17. Aoife inspects Lot 17 and is delighted with the quality of the collection. She is unaware that at least half of the glasses are not Waterford crystal but are imitations mass produced in China. Seán's son bought them for Seán as a joke during one of his trips to the East. Seán is aware of Aoife's mistake but ensures that he does not offer more than "fine crystal" in general terms. When Aoife tells him sympathetically that it must be hard to part with "his life's work", Seán responds merely by saying ''No, it is not". Aoife buys Lot 17 which is advertised merely as "Assorted Glassware". Six months later Aoife discovers the true origins of the Chinese glasses and claims that either the contract with Seán is invalid as a result of this mistake and that she is entitled to her money back, or that Seán is liable for failing to disclose the fact that the glasses were not all Waterford crystal. Aoife also bids €200 for a Waterford crystal chandelier owned by Tom. Although the chandelier is advertised as "without reserve", it is withdrawn from the auction when hers is the only bid. Kelly, the auctioneer, is acting on Tom's instructions. Two years later, Aoife reads that the chandelier has been sold for €40,000. She wishes to sue Torn for breach of contract. Advise Aoife.

questions.description: Ethan owns a business which produces organic jams and soups. Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Ethan owns a business which produces organic jams and soups. He enters into a contract with Catherine, a local farmer, for the purchase of 100 crates of organic fruit at a cost of €10,000. Clause 8 of the signed written contract states: "The buyer herby waives any implied statutory warranty of merchantability or of fitness for a particular purpose. In the event that any fruit sold is not of merchantable quality or fit for a particular purpose, we will refund all payments made to us by the buyer and this shall be the limit of our obligation. We hereby exclude all liability for any loss or damage arising from the use of any fruit supplied by us and for any consequential loss or damage arising out of such use." Catherine inadvertently sprays her fruit crop with chemical pesticides. When Ethan uses the fruit his jam is contaminated and he suffers a loss of €25,000 as a result. When Ethan sues Catherine for breach of contract, Catherine seeks to rely on Clause 8 to limit the damages payable to the €10,000 paid for the fruit. Each month for the last 5 years, Ethan has placed an order for organic vegetables with another local farmer, Paul. There is no express written contract between them but Ethan has told Paul on several occasions that he is "delighted with the arrangement" and that he "looks forward to developing the relationship in future years". Ethan has recently discovered that he can obtain organic vegetables at a lower price from another farmer and wishes to know if he can terminate the arrangement with Paul. Advise Ethan.

questions.description: Transport Shipping Ltd hired a ship to Grains Ltd in June 2013 for a hire… Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Transport Shipping Ltd hired a ship to Grains Ltd in June 2013 for a hire fee of €2,500 per day. The ship was due to be returned on 31st August 2013 and so Transport Shipping Ltd arranged to hire the same ship out to a third party, McKenna Ltd, for 90 days from 1st September 2013. Transport Shipping Ltd was able to negotiate an unusually high hire fee of €5,000 per day for this second hire period. Grains Ltd failed to return the ship until 10th September 2013. Grains Ltd admits it is in breach of contract and is willing to pay damages of €27,000 to cover an extra 10 days' hire at the current market rate of €2,700 per day. However as a result of the late delivery of the ship, Transport Shipping Ltd was forced to renegotiate its contract with McKenna Ltd to allow for a delayed start to the hire period and a reduced rate of hire of €2,000 per day. It thus claims that it has lost profits of €3,000 per day for the duration of the contract with McKenna Ltd, and wants damages of €270,000 to compensate for this loss. Grains Ltd argues that there is a "general understanding" in the shipping industry that damages should be limited to the market rate of hire until the ship is returned, and should not include any liability for any loss of future profits. Grains Ltd also argues that they were unaware and could not reasonably have been aware that Transport Shipping Ltd had entered into such a lucrative contract with McKenna Ltd. To avoid similar situations in the future, Transport Shipping Ltd wants to insert a clause in its contract providing for a set amount of compensation to be paid when there is a delay in returning a ship on time. It seeks your advice on whether and under what circumstances such a clause would be enforced by the courts. Advise Transport Shipping Ltd on its position regarding Grains Ltd and on the possibility of including such a clause in future.

questions.description: Dervla is a graphic designer who is concerned about health issues, in… Advise Dervla.
questions.text: Dervla is a graphic designer who is concerned about health issues, in particular the dangers of young people smoking. On her own initiative she designs a poster for "Tobac'', a charity that works to educate young people about the dangers of smoking. Edward, the public relations officer for Tobac, is delighted with the poster, and in an email to Dervla he writes: "We love your poster! As a token of our gratitude for your hard work, we would like to give you €1,000." Dervla replies "That's great, thank you!" and on the basis of this statement she buys herself a new state of the art laptop computer costing €950. However the next week Edward emails Dervla to say that owing to cutbacks they are no longer able to pay her any money. Dervla wants to know if there is any way she can claim the €1,000 promised. Dervia discovers that her 15 year old niece Anna has started smoking. Dervla tells Anna that if she quits smoking and does not smoke for six months that she will pay her €500. Six months later Anna contacts Dervla to let her know that she has successfully abstained from smoking for six months and that she wants her money. Dervla argues that she wasn't serious when she offered Anna the money; that she has not benefited from Anna's actions; and that it was only a promise to a young family member and not legally binding. She wants to know if she has to pay Anna the €500. Advise Dervla.

questions.description: Answer (a) or (b) (a) Discuss the main changes introduced by the implementation of the Consumer Rights Directive…
questions.text: Answer (a) or (b) (a) Discuss the main changes introduced by the implementation of the Consumer Rights Directive (Directive 2011/83/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council on Consumer Rights) in Ireland. [Or]{.underline} (b) Discuss, with reference to case law, the circumstances in which an order of specific performance is an appropriate remedy for breach of contract.

questions.description: Answer (a) or (b) (a) Discuss, with reference to case law, the grounds on which a contract may be held illegal on the…
questions.text: Answer (a) or (b) (a) Discuss, with reference to case law, the grounds on which a contract may be held illegal on the ground of public policy. [Or]{.underline} (b) Explain, with reference to case law, the elements necessary to establish that economic duress has vitiated consent to a contract.

questions.description: Answer (a) and (b) (a) Explain, with reference to case law, the circumstances in which an offer may be withdrawn or may…
questions.text: Answer (a) and (b) (a) Explain, with reference to case law, the circumstances in which an offer may be withdrawn or may otherwise be incapable of being accepted. [And]{.underline} (b) Discuss, with reference to case law, the circumstances in which it will be held that an apparent agreement is too vague or uncertain to be enforced.

questions.description: Why were sections 43 to 46 of the Sale of Goods and Supply of Services Act 1980 added to the Statute Book?
questions.text: Why were sections 43 to 46 of the Sale of Goods and Supply of Services Act 1980 added to the Statute Book? How important and successful have these provisions turned out to be?

questions.description: Máire decides to sell her car and buy a new one. Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Máire decides to sell her car and buy a new one. She knows her friend Jim is interested in her car, so she emails him, offering to sell him her car for €5,000, "the offer to be kept open until noon July 11th". She says that if Jim wants to buy the car, he should "indicate acceptance via email and pay a deposit of €500 before the deadline". Jim emails Máire to accept her offer on July 8th but does not send on a deposit. On July 9th Máire drops by Jim's house to tell him that she is revoking her offer and the car is no longer available to buy as she has agreed to sell it to her friend Sarah. Jim objects, saying that Máire impliedly promised not to sell the car to anyone else before noon on June 11th, and that he has already accepted her offer and she cannot now revoke it. Jim also tries to give her the €500 deposit in cash but Máire refuses to accept this. Jim is threatening to sue Máire for breach of contract. Máire goes shopping for a new car. She visits her local car dealer, Andy, and sees a car she likes in the forecourt, with a price tag of €8,000. However when she goes into the shop to buy the car, Andy tells her that there was a mistake on the price tag and the car actually costs €10,000. Maeve wants to know if she can insist on Andy selling her the car for €8,000. Advise Máire.

questions.description: In May 2014 he builds his neighbour Max a new garden shed as a surprise… Advise Terry.
questions.text: Terry is a builder. In May 2014 he builds his neighbour Max a new garden shed as a surprise birthday present. Max is delighted when he sees the completed shed and says "I insist on paying you a grand for this work." However, he fails to pay Terry any money, and when Terry asks him for the money Max says that he doesn't have a legally binding obligation to pay him the €1,000. In June 2014, Terry completes a building contract for Emma and requests payment of the outstanding balance of €200,000. His contract with Emma states that this sum is due upon completion. By August 2014, Emma still has not paid, claiming she has "cash flow" problems, and Terry is in desperate financial straits. He requests that Emma pay him €150,000 immediately "in full settlement" of their account. Emma agrees to this and immediately pays him this amount, by cheque. The cheque has been cashed but Terry subsequently sues Emma for the outstanding balance plus interest. Emma argues that Terry is estopped from making such a claim but Terry insists that his promise to accept €150,000 in full settlement of the debt is unenforceable. Advise Terry.

questions.description: Felix is a struggling artist who works from his home, a house which he… Advise Felix.
questions.text: Felix is a struggling artist who works from his home, a house which he inherited from his grandmother. He is severely depressed and on strong anti-depressant medication which leaves him drowsy. Felix's boyfriend Sam runs a music shop in town, but Felix does not know that the shop is rapidly losing money. Sam wants to take out a business loan of €100,000 to cover his losses, but needs Felix to act as guarantor and to charge his interest in the home as security for the loan. Sam tells Felix that the loan is simply to cover the cost of an extension which he is building on the premises, and that it is a loan of €10,000. Felix is very insecure about their relationship, and is worried that Sam is about to leave him. He agrees to act as guarantor, and charges his interest in the home to the bank as security. In the bank he signs a form which states that he has received independent advice from a solicitor that the loan is for €100,000, but Felix is flustered and does not read over the details of the loan. A year later Sam's business fails completely and he disappears. The bank is attempting to take possession of Felix's house. Advise Felix.

questions.description: Natalia runs a small business in which she takes groups of tourists out… Damages.
questions.text: Natalia runs a small business in which she takes groups of tourists out onto a local lake for short cruises. She buys a second hand boat from Andy, and during the course of negotiations she mentions that she will be using the boat for this purpose. However, one day, a month after buying the boat, the engine appears to malfunction - it becomes very loud and starts to emit fumes and black smoke. Her clients begin to complain and some start to look for their money back after the trip. The fumes are so bad that she is forced to stop using the boat and, as she cannot get a replacement, she has to cancel several lake cruises which had been booked. When she asks Andy to repair the boat, he says that the boat was old and that he never warranted that the boat would be of satisfactory quality. Natalia wants Andy to repair the boat, or failing that to give her a refund. She also wants damages to compensate for the tours which were cancelled and those where she had to provide a refund. Advise Natalia.

questions.description: In what circumstances will a party to a contract be entitled to regard itself as discharged from contractual…
questions.text: In what circumstances will a party to a contract be entitled to regard itself as discharged from contractual obligations as a result of a breach of the contract by the other party?

questions.description: Explain, with reference to case law, how a court distinguishes between a term of a contract, a representation, and a…
questions.text: Explain, with reference to case law, how a court distinguishes between a term of a contract, a representation, and a "mere advertising puff"? What are the consequences of these distinctions?

questions.description: Answer (a) AND (b).
questions.text: Answer (a) AND (b). Each question carries equal marks. (a)Discuss the approach taken by the Irish courts to a plea that a contract was entered into because of a common mistake of the contracting parties. And (b)Discuss whether equitable relief should be available for common mistake.

questions.description: Answer (a) OR (b) (a) Discuss the legislative provisions which protect Irish consumers from unfair contract terms and…
questions.text: Answer (a) OR (b) (a) Discuss the legislative provisions which protect Irish consumers from unfair contract terms and exclusion clauses. Refer to relevant case law in your answer. (b) Explain the circumstances in which a third party may take the benefit of an exemption clause in a contract to which he or she is not a party. Is the law in Ireland in need of reform?
